context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help figuring out this reflection prompt tbh. I’m stuck on what “development” even means today, and how to turn that into a clear argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before we dig into the prompt — what do YOU think development means? Just off the top of your head, no pressure to be precise.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and needs to engage with the core question themselves. Rather than scaffolding the concept of development for them, I should ask a probing Socratic question that gets them to articulate their own initial understanding first. This follows guided discovery and keeps answers bite-sized.

exchanges.student: My first thought is it means economic growth, but that feels too narrow rn. I think it also includes quality of life stuff, like health, education, and maybe political power too.
exchanges.tutor: That's a productive tension you've spotted. So if development has expanded from just economic growth to include health, education, political power... does adding all those dimensions make the concept stronger, or does it risk meaning everything and nothing at once?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good initial observation - recognizing the tension between narrow economic definitions and broader conceptions of development. This is actually at the heart of the prompt. I should affirm the direction of their thinking and push them further with a Socratic question that connects to the prompt's key challenge: has this broadening made 'development' meaningless?

exchanges.student: Yeah that’s the part confusing me lol. I wanna say it gets stronger bc it’s more realistic, but it also kinda risks becoming so broad that it’s hard to measure or argue about.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the tension the prompt is asking you to wrestle with. Now — which side do you actually lean toward? Your reflection needs an argument, not just a both-sides observation. What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the core tension in the prompt. This is good progress. I should acknowledge this and push them to take a position - the prompt asks them to argue one way or the other, so I want to push them toward committing to a stance rather than sitting on the fence.

exchanges.student: I think I lean toward “it still has a core meaning,” just not a super narrow one. Like, maybe the core is improving human well-being and people’s ability to shape their lives, not just GDP.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid stance to build your argument around. Now the prompt pushes further — if that's your definition of success in development, which countries or communities would you point to as role models, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a defensible position that echoes concepts like the human development approach (Sen's capabilities, HDI, etc.) without necessarily naming them. This is solid enough to build an argument on. I should affirm the direction and push them to the next part of the prompt: success and role models.

exchanges.student: Maybe Costa Rica or Kerala? I’m not 100% sure tho, but I picked them bc they seem strong on health/education without being the richest.
exchanges.tutor: Interesting picks. What specifically about Costa Rica or Kerala connects back to your core argument about well-being and people's ability to shape their lives? Try to make that link explicit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good choices that align with their stated definition of development. Rather than validating or questioning the choices directly, I should push them to articulate WHY these examples support their argument - connecting the examples back to their core claim. This moves the reflection forward meaningfully.

exchanges.student: I think the link is that they show development can mean strong social outcomes, not just high income. But idk how much to say about the “ability to shape their lives” part exactly.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way — in the places you mentioned, do ordinary people have meaningful say over decisions that affect them? What would that look like concretely?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good connection but is uncertain about the political agency/empowerment dimension of their argument. Rather than explaining it for them, I should ask a question that helps them think through what 'ability to shape their lives' actually looks like in practice, which will help them develop that part of their argument themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe through democracy, local participation, or access to public services that give ppl more real choices? I’m kinda guessing tho, so can u help me make that more concrete?
exchanges.tutor: You're not guessing — those are real dimensions worth exploring. Rather than me filling that in, try this: pick ONE of those three things (democracy, local participation, public services) and think about whether your chosen examples actually demonstrate it. What do you know about them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them - to make their argument more concrete. I should redirect this back to them while keeping them on track. They're actually close to something solid already. I should push them to connect their examples (Costa Rica/Kerala) specifically to the concrete dimensions they just listed rather than giving them content.

exchanges.student: Public services feels easiest rn. I know Costa Rica is known for healthcare and education, and I think Kerala is too, so maybe that shows development as expanding real life chances.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a concrete link you can use. You've now got material for the first two parts of the prompt. What about the third part — the global crisis question? Too much markets, too much government, too little of either, and at what scale?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid connection between their examples and their argument. We've now covered the first two parts of the prompt reasonably well. To avoid redundancy and keep moving toward a complete reflection, I should redirect them to the third and final part of the prompt about the global crisis - markets vs. government, and scale.

exchanges.student: I’m leaning too much market tbh, esp if basic needs get left to markets alone. But I’m not sure what to say about “what scale” besides national govts matter a lot.
exchanges.tutor: National governments matter, sure — but think about the 2008 financial crisis for a second. Was that a problem that individual national governments could solve alone? What other scales might matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable position on the markets vs. government question. On scale, they've defaulted to the national level, which is a starting point but undersells the complexity. A Socratic nudge about other scales (global, local) should help them think more richly about this without me giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: Oh wait, yeah not really lol. So maybe it’s too much market plus too little regulation at both national and global scales, since finance crosses borders.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and notice how that connects back to your earlier point about who has the power to shape people's lives. You now have positions on all three parts of the prompt. Try drafting your opening paragraph — what's your central argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a more sophisticated position on scale by reasoning through it themselves. This is good progress. We've now covered all three parts of the prompt across roughly 9 exchanges. I should push the student to start integrating everything into a written attempt, as per the redundancy/integration rule and the fact that we're near the end of the planned conversation length.
